mission.title: Install and Configure Kubean on Kubernetes
mission.description: Kubean is a production-ready cluster lifecycle management toolchain based on Kubespray, designed to simplify the deployment and management of Kubernetes clusters. Installing Kubean allows you to leverage its powerful lifecycle management features for your Kubernetes environments.

steps.title: Add the Kubean Helm repository
steps.description:
Add the official Kubean Helm chart repository to your Helm configuration.
```bash
helm repo add kubean https://kubean-io.github.io/kubean/charts
helm repo update
```

steps.title: Install Kubean using Helm
steps.description:
Install Kubean with a specific version and create a namespace for it.
```bash
helm install kubean kubean/kubean --version 0.32.4 --namespace kubean --create-namespace
```

steps.title: Verify the installation
steps.description:
Check if the Kubean pods are running successfully in the kubean namespace.
```bash
kubectl get pods -n kubean
```

steps.title: Configure resource limits for Kubean operator
steps.description:
Update the Kubean operator deployment to set resource limits. You can edit the Helm release to include resource limits.
```bash
helm upgrade kubean kubean/kubean --namespace kubean --set kubeanOperator.resources.limits.cpu=500m --set kubeanOperator.resources.limits.memory=512Mi
```

steps.title: Set up RBAC for Kubean
steps.description:
Ensure that the necessary RBAC permissions are set up for Kubean. You can create a service account and bind it to the cluster role.
```bash
kubectl create serviceaccount kubean-sa -n kubean
kubectl create clusterrolebinding kubean-sa --clusterrole=cluster-admin --serviceaccount=kubean:kubean-sa
```

resolution.summary: A successful installation of Kubean will show all pods running in the kubean namespace when you execute `kubectl get pods -n kubean`. Additionally, the resource limits and RBAC configurations will be applied as specified.
codeSnippets:
helm repo add kubean https://kubean-io.github.io/kubean/charts
helm repo update
helm upgrade kubean kubean/kubean --namespace kubean --set kubeanOperator.resources.limits.cpu=500m --set kubeanOperator.resources.limits.memory=512Mi

kubectl create serviceaccount kubean-sa -n kubean
kubectl create clusterrolebinding kubean-sa --clusterrole=cluster-admin --serviceaccount=kubean:kubean-sa

uninstall.title: Remove the Helm release
uninstall.description: Uninstall the Kubean Helm release to remove the deployment from your cluster.

uninstall.title: Clean up CRDs and persistent resources
uninstall.description: Delete any Custom Resource Definitions (CRDs) and persistent volume claims (PVCs) associated with Kubean.

uninstall.title: Remove namespace and verify
uninstall.description: Delete the Kubean namespace and verify that all resources have been removed.

upgrade.title: Backup current state
upgrade.description: Before upgrading, back up the current state of your Kubean installation.

upgrade.title: Update repo and run upgrade command
upgrade.description: Update the Helm repository and run the upgrade command to install the new version of Kubean.

upgrade.title: Verify the upgrade
upgrade.description: Check the status of the Kubean pods to ensure they are running the new version.

troubleshooting.title: Pods stuck in CrashLoopBackOff
troubleshooting.description:
This issue occurs when a pod fails to start properly and keeps crashing. To diagnose, check the logs of the pod using the following command:
```bash
kubectl logs <pod-name> -n kubean
```
Look for any error messages that indicate what might be causing the crash.

troubleshooting.title: Helm upgrade fails with version conflict
troubleshooting.description:
If you encounter a version conflict during an upgrade, it may be due to incompatible changes. Check the current version with:
```bash
helm list -n kubean
```
Then, specify the exact version to upgrade to using the `--version` flag.

troubleshooting.title: RBAC permission errors
troubleshooting.description:
If you see permission denied errors, ensure that the service account has the correct permissions. Verify the service account and role binding with:
```bash
kubectl get serviceaccount kubean-sa -n kubean
kubectl get clusterrolebinding kubean-sa
```
If necessary, recreate the role binding.

troubleshooting.title: Kubean operator not responding
troubleshooting.description:
If the Kubean operator is unresponsive, check its logs for errors:
```bash
kubectl logs -l app=kubean-operator -n kubean
```
Investigate any errors and ensure that the operator pod is running.

prerequisites.description: You need a Kubernetes cluster running version 1.24 or higher, along with Helm and kubectl installed on your local machine.
